synthesis.notes: 5-AGNRs are synthesized from 3,9(10)-dibromoperylene (DBP) monomer via surface-assisted dehalogenation, polymerization, and cyclodehydrogenation on a metal surface by CVD at 350 °C. By increasing the annealing temperature to 600 °C, 5-AGNRs can undergo lateral fusion into wider GNRs (a mixture of 10-AGNR and non-negligible amounts of 5-AGNR, 15-AGNR, and 20-AGNR). The resulting GNR films have an average length of about 10 nm, with the longest extending up to 35 nm.

steps.description: CVD synthesis of 9-AGNR by surface-assisted dehalogenation and polymerization of DBTP monomer on a metal surface under Ar and H2 gas flow at ~1.5 mbar pressure in a tube furnace.

equipment.name: tube furnace
equipment.settings: Ar 500 sccm, H2 100 sccm, pressure ~1.5 mbar

steps.description: Annealing at 400°C to induce polymerization and cyclodehydrogenation (graphitization) of the polymer to form 9-AGNRs.
Cover the GNR film with a PMMA film to provide mechanical support and protect the GNRs for transfer.
Detach the mica substrate by immersion in hydrofluoric acid (40 wt.%) solution.
Remove the gold layer below the GNRs using a commercial gold etchant.
Clean the GNR/PMMA film in ultra-pure water several times.
Transfer the GNR/PMMA film onto the target device with pre-fabricated graphene electrodes.
Remove the PMMA support layer by hot acetone washing.
Anneal the graphene/GNR devices at 375 K for 1 hour in vacuum before electrical transport characterization.

synthesis.notes: 9-AGNR was synthesized by CVD using DBTP as monomer at an annealing temperature of 400°C. For comparison, 9-AGNR was also synthesized by UHV method (2x10^-10 mbar) from DITP on Au(111)/mica surface, yielding longer ribbons (~45 nm average length vs ~10 nm for CVD). The CVD-grown GNRs have an average length of ~10 nm with the longest up to 35 nm. The UHV synthesis involved: Au(111)/mica cleaning by sputtering (1 kV Ar+ for 10 min) and annealing (470°C for 10 min); monomer sublimation at 70°C for 2 min onto Au(111) at room temperature (~0.5 monolayer coverage); polymerization at 200°C for 10 min; cyclodehydrogenation at 400°C for 10 min.

steps.description: CVD synthesis is performed in a tube furnace under gas flow of Ar (500 sccm) and H2 (100 sccm) with pressure of ~1.5 mbar using DBP as the monomer precursor.
5-AGNR is first synthesized from DBP at an annealing temperature of 350 °C via surface-assisted dehalogenation, polymerization, and cyclodehydrogenation.
Further annealing of the 5-AGNR to 600 °C induces lateral fusion into wider GNRs, resulting in a mixture of 10-AGNR and non-negligible amounts of 5-AGNR, 15-AGNR, and 20-AGNR (labelled 5n-AGNR).

synthesis.notes: 5n-AGNR is a mixture containing mainly 10-AGNR and also some amounts of 5-, 15-, and 20-AGNRs, produced by lateral fusion of 5-AGNR at elevated annealing temperature (600 °C). The width-specific radial breathing-like mode (RBLM) of Raman spectra confirms the presence of GNRs with multiple widths. The GNR films are made by a dense layer of GNRs with average length of about 10 nm, with the longest extending up to 35 nm.

synthesis.notes: The paper describes synthesis of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) which are carbon-based nanomaterials. Two methods are described: CVD method for 5-AGNR, 9-AGNR, and 5n-AGNR; and UHV method for 9-AGNR. The GNRs are synthesized from dihalogenated monomers (DBTP, DBP, DITP) through surface-assisted polymerization and cyclodehydrogenation. Average GNR length is ~10 nm for CVD-grown and ~45 nm for UHV-grown samples. The synthesized GNR films are transferred onto graphene electrodes for device fabrication.
